nodes.name: Countries of the World
nodes.description: Country sets by region and category

config.prompt: Name all 12 sovereign countries of South America

items.explanation:
Second-largest country in South America; capital is Buenos Aires
Eighth-largest country in the world by area (~2.78 million km²)
Home to Patagonia, Tierra del Fuego, and the Andes along its western border
Won FIFA World Cup in 1978, 1986 (Maradona's 'Hand of God'), and 2022 (Messi)
Tango originated in the working-class neighborhoods of Buenos Aires in the late 19th century

Landlocked country in central South America; constitutional capital is Sucre, seat of government is La Paz
La Paz sits at ~3,640 m elevation — the highest de facto capital city in the world
Named after Simón Bolívar, who led independence movements across South America
Lost its Pacific coast to Chile in the War of the Pacific (1879–1884) and still claims maritime access
Home to the Salar de Uyuni, the world's largest salt flat (~10,582 km²)

Largest country in South America and fifth-largest in the world (~8.5 million km²); capital is Brasília
Only Portuguese-speaking country in the Americas; shares a border with every South American country except Chile and Ecuador
Holds the record for most FIFA World Cup wins (5: 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002)
The Amazon rainforest covers ~60% of its territory and contains roughly 10% of all species on Earth
Brasília was purpose-built as the capital in 1960, designed by Oscar Niemeyer and Lúcio Costa

Long, narrow country along South America's western coast; capital is Santiago
Stretches ~4,300 km north to south but averages only ~177 km wide — one of the world's longest and narrowest countries
Home to the Atacama Desert, the driest nonpolar desert on Earth; some weather stations there have never recorded rain
Easter Island (Rapa Nui), famous for its moai statues, is Chilean territory ~3,700 km offshore in the Pacific
Gained its northern desert territory from Bolivia and Peru in the War of the Pacific (1879–1884)

Located in northwestern South America; capital is Bogotá
Only South American country with coastlines on both the Caribbean Sea and the Pacific Ocean
Named after Christopher Columbus, though he never set foot in the country
Produces more emeralds than any other country — roughly 70–90% of the world's supply
Colombian author Gabriel García Márquez won the Nobel Prize in Literature (1982) for works including *One Hundred Years of Solitude*

Located on South America's northwest coast, straddling the equator; capital is Quito
Named directly after the equator — the only country in the world named for a geographic line
Smallest of the Andean nations (~283,561 km²)
The Galápagos Islands, ~960 km off the coast, inspired Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection
Quito's historic center, at ~2,850 m elevation, was one of the first UNESCO World Heritage Sites (1978)

Located on the northern coast of South America; capital is Georgetown
Only English-speaking country on the South American mainland
Formerly British Guiana; gained independence in 1966
Name comes from an indigenous Amerindian word meaning 'Land of Many Waters'
Site of the Jonestown massacre (1978), in which over 900 members of Jim Jones's Peoples Temple cult died in a mass murder-suicide

Landlocked country in central South America; capital is Asunción
One of the few officially bilingual countries in the Americas — both Spanish and Guaraní are official languages, with ~90% of the population speaking Guaraní
Lost an estimated 60–70% of its population in the War of the Triple Alliance (1864–1870) against Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay
The Itaipú Dam on the Paraná River (shared with Brazil) was the world's largest hydroelectric dam by output until China's Three Gorges surpassed it
Asunción, founded in 1537, is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in South America

Located on South America's western coast; capital is Lima
Home to Machu Picchu, the 15th-century Inca citadel and one of the New Seven Wonders of the World
The Amazon rainforest covers ~60% of Peru's territory, making it the second-largest share of the Amazon after Brazil
Lima was founded by Francisco Pizarro in 1535 and served as the capital of Spain's South American empire
The Nazca Lines — massive geoglyphs etched into the desert floor ~2,000 years ago — are visible only from the air and remain partly unexplained

Located on the northeast coast of South America; capital is Paramaribo
Smallest sovereign country in South America by area (~163,820 km²)
Only sovereign state outside Europe where Dutch is the official language
Over 90% of its territory is covered by tropical rainforest — the highest proportion of forest cover of any country
Extremely diverse ethnically: no majority group; has some of the largest Hindu and Muslim populations in the Americas proportionally

Located on South America's southeastern coast between Argentina and Brazil; capital is Montevideo
Smallest Spanish-speaking country in South America (~176,215 km²)
Hosted and won the first FIFA World Cup in 1930; won again in 1950 (the 'Maracanazo' upset over Brazil)
One of the most politically stable and socially progressive countries in Latin America — first in South America to legalize same-sex marriage (2013)
About half the population (~1.8 million of ~3.5 million) lives in the Montevideo metropolitan area

Located on the northern coast of South America on the Caribbean; capital is Caracas
Name means 'Little Venice' — Spanish explorers saw indigenous stilt houses on Lake Maracaibo and were reminded of Venice
Home to Angel Falls (Salto Ángel), the world's highest uninterrupted waterfall at 979 m
Sits atop some of the world's largest proven oil reserves; oil wealth fueled rapid urbanization in the 20th century
The Catatumbo lightning phenomenon over Lake Maracaibo produces lightning ~300 nights per year — the highest lightning density on Earth
